quote: In the beginner’s mind there are many possibilities, but in the expert’s mind there are few.
through_line: Friday slows to a different tempo. The Aeon piece on AI doing science is not a warning but a quiet provocation: when a mind radically different from ours generates knowledge, we may not be able to verify it or even fully understand it. The Psyche piece on meditation and mountains asks for the same humility in a different register — that some territories require careful preparation, not just courage. Smithsonian’s science stories give the week a wide-horizon close.

articles.title: When AIs do science, it will be strange and incomprehensible
articles.pitch: The essay’s argument is precise: if AI systems begin generating scientific knowledge autonomously, using methods and representations with no human-readable analogue, the verification process breaks down — not because the knowledge is wrong, but because we may have no way to confirm or understand it. A genuinely unsettling thought experiment about the epistemology of post-human science.

articles.title: Meditation is like mountaineering: approach it with care
articles.pitch: A piece that takes the risks of intensive meditation seriously — not as a reason to avoid the practice but as a call for the same careful preparation that any serious physical or psychological undertaking requires. The mountaineering analogy is apt without being reductive, and the piece is honest about the range of experiences meditation can produce.

articles.title: The Ten Most Significant Science Stories of 2025, From Medical Breakthroughs to an Interstellar Visitor
articles.pitch: Smithsonian’s editors select the ten science stories that most significantly shaped 2025 — from medical breakthroughs to an interstellar visitor. Wide-ranging, accessible, and a reminder that the year contained more than its headlines suggested. A good Friday read.
